key_strengths: World's largest P&C insurer with record 85.7% combined ratio
Multi-layered moat: switching costs, global scale, regulatory barriers, AA ratings
Owner earnings of $10.6B with 20.5% return on tangible equity
21-year CEO track record with disciplined capital allocation
Fortress balance sheet: 23% debt/equity, 18x interest coverage
key_risks: Catastrophe clustering ($10B+ insured loss year could compress earnings 40-50%)
P&C cycle softening eroding premium growth and combined ratio
Casualty reserve deterioration from social inflation and US litigation trends
CEO succession risk after 21+ years with no public succession plan
Climate regulation constraining underwriting in exposed regions
red_flags: Combined Chairman/CEO role concentrates governance risk
Commercial auto combined ratio of 126.2% is a sub-segment drag
As You Sow climate lawsuit filed March 2026
buy_triggers: Stock pulls back to $280-$300 (near MC P5-P10, 25-30% discount to base IV)
Combined ratio sustains below 86% for another year
Hard-market pricing extends through 2027 supporting 6%+ premium growth
sell_triggers: Combined ratio exceeds 95% for two consecutive years
Management pivots to aggressive M&A at elevated prices
Stock exceeds bull IV ($594) or trades above 18x trailing earnings
valuation_summary: Base intrinsic value of $393/share implies 22% upside from $322.58; Monte Carlo simulation shows 89% probability of undervaluation across 10,000 runs, with 24 of 25 sensitivity grid cells above current price.
compact_checklist: Chubb collects $54.8B in annual insurance premiums across 54 countries and invests the float, earning money two ways — underwriting profit (85.7% combined ratio) and $7B in investment income on $272B in assets.

Switching costs in complex commercial lines, global scale 2x the nearest competitor, AA financial strength ratings, and 54-country regulatory licenses create a multi-layered moat evidenced by a record combined ratio 9-14 points better than the industry average.
Return on tangible equity of 20.5%, owner earnings of $10.6B (up 93% in three years), and FCF conversion averaging 163% confirm Chubb generates real cash well above its 6.8% cost of capital.

CEO Greenberg has compounded value for 21 years, bought back $3.7B in shares at $283 average (below today's $323), raised dividends for 33 consecutive years, and ties 95% of his pay to book value growth and combined ratio.
With 23% debt/equity, 18x interest coverage, $73.8B in equity, and $1.13B in favorable reserve development, Chubb could retire its entire $17.2B debt stack in 16 months of operating cash flow.

At 12.6x trailing earnings with a base IV of $393, the market must believe premium growth stalls at 4%, the combined ratio reverts 2-3 points worse, and WACC stays above 7.5% — all simultaneously — to justify $323.

89% Monte Carlo probability of undervaluation, 24 of 25 sensitivity grid cells above current price, and a business that absorbed $2.4B in catastrophe losses while posting record earnings provide structural downside protection.
Sell if the combined ratio exceeds 95% for two consecutive years, signaling a permanent loss of the underwriting discipline that defines Chubb's entire competitive advantage.
source_summary: Analysis based on Chubb Q4/FY2025 earnings, 10-K filing, Yahoo Finance financials, quant DCF model from src/quant, and web search for current data as of 2026-03-22.
